perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information shall not include information that: a. is or becomes known publicly through no fault of the Recipient; or b. is learned by the Recipient from a third party not under an obligation of confi- dence to the Discloser and who has legitimate and legal possession of the informa- tion; or c. is already known to the Recipient before receipt from the Discloser as can be proven by written records; or d. is independently developed by employees or consultants of the Recipient who did not have access to any Confidential Information received; e. is free of confidentiality restrictions by written agreement of the Discloser.
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information shall not include information that: a. is or becomes known publicly through no fault of the Recipient; or b. is learned by the Recipient from a third party who may or may not have had an obligation of confidence to the Discloser and who may or may not have had legitimate and legal possession of the informa- tion; or c. is already known to the Recipient and can be proven by a preponderance of the evidence; or d. is independently developed by employees or consultants of the Recipient, irrespective of whether they had access to any Confidential Information received; e. is free of confidentiality restrictions with or without written agreement of the Discloser.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text introduces ambiguity regarding what constitutes an exception to 'Confidential Information'. By weakening the requirements for excluding information from confidential status, it creates uncertainty about the scope of protection, particularly concerning third-party disclosures, prior knowledge, and independent development. Removing the 'written agreement' requirement further exacerbates this.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modifications contradict the UTSA's definition of 'misappropriation,' which hinges on the recipient's knowledge or reason to know that the information was acquired through improper means or under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy. By removing the requirement of a third party's obligation of confidence and legitimate possession, the changed text weakens the protection against misappropriation, effectively allowing the use of trade secrets obtained through questionable means. Similarly, removing the need for a written agreement with the Discloser also contradicts the UTSA, as it implies a more relaxed standard for releasing confidentiality restrictions, undermining the Discloser's control over their trade secrets.

perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the UTSA (Uniform Trade Secrets Act) by weakening the protection against misappropriation. The original text requires a third party to be under an obligation of confidence and have legitimate possession of the information for it to be excluded from confidential information. The changed text weakens this by stating the third party 'may or may not' have those obligations. This directly contradicts the intent of the UTSA, which aims to protect trade secrets from being acquired through improper means. Furthermore, the removal of the 'written agreement' requirement for releasing confidentiality restrictions undermines the discloser's control over their trade secrets, further contradicting the UTSA's goals.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party warrants that it has the right to disclose all of the Confidential Information it discloses, but does not otherwise make any representation as to the ac-curacy, suitability, or completeness of such Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party warrants that it has the right to disclose all of the Confidential Information it discloses, and also makes every representation as to the ac-curacy, suitability, and completeness of such Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: The original text explicitly disclaims any representation regarding the accuracy, suitability, or completeness of the disclosed information. By modifying it to assert that every such representation is made, it creates a contradiction and potential liability. This would violate the principle of 'caveat emptor' or 'buyer beware'.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Warranty Laws under the Uniform Commercial Code (UCC)
perturbation.law_explanation: By asserting that a party makes every representation as to accuracy, suitability, and completeness, the modified text eliminates any disclaimer of warranties and potentially invokes implied warranty provisions under the UCC, even though this agreement concerns confidential information disclosure rather than the sale of goods. UCC § 2-316 outlines how implied warranties can be excluded or modified. The original language explicitly excludes these warranties, but the modified language does the opposite, opening the door to potential breach of warranty claims based on the disclosed information.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-316. Exclusion or Modification of Warranties. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-316. Exclusion or Modification of Warranties. (1) Words or conduct relevant to the creation of an express warranty and words or conduct tending to negate or limit warranty shall be construed wherever reasonable as consistent with each other; but subject to the provisions of this Article on parol or extrinsic evidence (Section 2-202 ) negation or limitation is inoperative to the extent that such construction is unreasonable. (2)Subject to subsection (3), to exclude or modify the implied warranty of merchantability or any part of it the language must mention merchantability and in case of a writing must be conspicuous, and to exclude or modify any implied warranty of fitness the exclusion must be by a writing and conspicuous. Language to exclude all implied warranties of fitness is sufficient if it states, for example, that "There are no warranties which extend beyond the description on the face hereof." (3)Notwithstanding subsection (2) (a) unless the circumstances indicate otherwise, all implied warranties are excluded by expressions like "as is", "with all faults" or other language which in common understanding calls the buyer's attention to the exclusion of warranties and makes plain that there is no implied warranty; and (b) when the buyer before entering into the contract has examined the goods or the sample or model as fully as he desired or has refused to examine the goods there is no implied warranty with regard to defects which an examination ought in the circumstances to have revealed to him; and (c) an implied warranty can also be excluded or modified by course of dealing or course of performance or usage of trade. (4) Remedies for breach of warranty can be limited in accordance with the provisions of this Article on liquidation or limitation of damages and on contractual modification of remed
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly disclaims warranties regarding the accuracy, suitability, or completeness of confidential information. The changed text asserts that every representation is made regarding these aspects. This directly contradicts the original disclaimer and, according to the Law Explanation, could invoke implied warranty provisions under the UCC, even though the agreement concerns confidential information. UCC § 2-316 allows for the exclusion or modification of warranties. By removing the original disclaimer and explicitly making representations, the modified text creates a legal contradiction, opening the door to potential breach of warranty claims, which the original text sought to avoid.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party agrees that the wrongful disclosure of Confidential In- Formation may cause irreparable injury that is inadequately compensable by monetary damages. As such, either Party may seek injunctive relief in any court of competent jurisdiction for the breach and/or the threatened breach of this Agreement in addition to any other remedies it may seek at law or equity, and the Recipient will not raise the defense of an adequate remedy at law.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party agrees that the wrongful disclosure of Confidential In- Formation may or may not cause irreparable injury that is inadequately compensable by monetary damages. As such, either Party may or may not seek injunctive relief in any court of competent jurisdiction for the breach and/or the threatened breach of this Agreement in addition to any other remedies it may seek at law or equity, and the Recipient may raise the defense of an adequate remedy at law.
perturbation.explanation: The original text establishes that wrongful disclosure causes irreparable harm and waives the defense of adequate remedy at law. The modified text makes the irreparable harm and availability of injunctive relief uncertain by including 'may or may not'. The Recipient is now able to raise the defense of an adequate remedy at law, making it harder for the Discloser to seek injunctive relief.
perturbation.law_citation: eBay Inc. v. MercExchange, L.L.C., 547 U.S. 388 (2006)
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts the established legal standard for obtaining injunctive relief, particularly as clarified in cases like *eBay Inc. v. MercExchange, L.L.C.*, which requires a showing of irreparable harm. By introducing uncertainty as to whether wrongful disclosure *may or may not* cause irreparable injury, and by allowing the defense of an adequate remedy at law, the change undermines the ability to obtain an injunction, deviating from the traditional requirements for such equitable relief. Injunctive relief is the main remedy for trade secrets.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 404 | Berkman Klein Center Skip to the main content Page Cannot Be Found Brave traveler, your journey on our site has brought you to an abrupt dead end. Perhaps you wish to explore one of these other options? About Us Get Involved Events Community Publications Projects & Tools Last updated Mar 27, 2024
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts established legal standards regarding irreparable harm and the availability of injunctive relief. The original text waived the defense of adequate remedy at law, while the modified text allows the Recipient to raise this defense, making it harder to seek injunctive relief. While the scraped snippets are not useful, the law explanation provides sufficient justification for a contradiction because the changed text undermines the ability to obtain an injunction, deviating from the requirements for such equitable relief. The change introduces uncertainty, creating ambiguity where there was none. This aligns with the Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction definition, because the change undermines established legal concepts related to injunctive relief by using language that deviates from legal standards. The original text was based on the premise that an injunction was necessary because monetary damages would not be sufficient to cover the harm. By stating that the disclosure 'may or may not' cause irreparable injury, and the recipient 'may' raise the defense of adequate remedy at law, the modified text makes it much harder to receive injunctive relief and thus creates a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be construed in accordance with the laws of the State of [state] without regard to any conflict of law principles.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be construed in accordance with the laws of the State of [state] with complete regard to any conflict of law principles.
perturbation.explanation: By changing the governing law clause to 'with complete regard to any conflict of law principles,' the contract now mandates that the court apply conflict of laws rules to determine which jurisdiction's law applies. This contradicts the original intention of specifying a single jurisdiction.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification undermines the purpose of a choice-of-law clause. While parties are generally free to choose the governing law of their contract, mandating 'complete regard to any conflict of law principles' introduces uncertainty. Section 187 of the Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws addresses the law governing contractual issues; however, it also describes the principle that the chosen law is generally followed unless it is contrary to a fundamental policy of a state which has a materially greater interest than the chosen state. With the modified text, the court now has to examine the laws of all states with a material interest. This contradicts the goal of clearly identifying which state's laws should be applied, and can potentially invalidate the choice of law provision entirely.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: conflict of laws | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you conflict of laws Conflict of laws refers to a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute . The conflicting legal rules may come from U.S. federal law, the laws of U.S. states, or the laws of other countries. The process by which a court determines what law to apply is sometimes referred to as "characterization" or "classification." This determination must be made in accordance with the law of the forum . A federal court in a case before it based on diversity of citizenship , for example, determines the conflict of law issue as if it were the highest court in the state in which it is sitting. Courts faced with a choice of law issue generally have two choices: A court can apply the law of the forum ( lex fori ) -- which is usually the result when the question of what law to apply is procedural . Or the court can apply the law of the site of the transaction or occurrence that gave rise to the litigation in the first place ( lex loci ) -- this is usually the controlling law selected when the matter is substantive . Federal courts play by different rules than state courts because federal jurisdiction is limited to what has been enumerated in the Constitution . The rules that federal courts must obey regarding which laws to apply are extremely complex. perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the original intention of specifying a single jurisdiction's law to govern the agreement. By mandating 'complete regard to any conflict of law principles,' the modified contract introduces uncertainty and compels the court to consider the laws of multiple jurisdictions. This undermines the purpose of the choice-of-law clause, as explained in the Law Explanation, and is supported by the Wex definition of 'conflict of laws,' which refers to 'a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute .' The original clause aimed to avoid this very situation.
